text.title: WebSocket Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Common WebSocket mistakes: missing heartbeat, unbounded connections, no auth, blocking handlers.

antipattern.description: No heartbeat: connections sit idle and the server never detects stale connections.
antipattern.reason: Stale connections accumulate and consume file descriptors. On Linux, the default `nofile` limit is 1024. 10k idle connections exhaust the limit and block new connections.

antipattern.description: No per-connection or per-user limit on concurrent connections.
antipattern.reason: A single user or bot can open thousands of connections, exhausting server resources and starving legitimate users.

antipattern.description: No authentication on the upgrade handshake — anonymous connections accepted, authorization checked after the first message.
antipattern.reason: Unauthenticated connections consume resources before rejection. If auth is checked after the first message, an attacker can open thousands of connections without sending any data.

antipattern.description: Blocking or CPU-intensive operations inside async message handlers.
antipattern.reason: Async WebSocket handlers run on the event loop. A blocking call (file read, CPU hash, synchronous DB) stalls all other WebSocket connections on the same worker until it completes.

antipattern.description: Sending large payloads (tens of KB or more) directly in WebSocket messages.
antipattern.reason: Large messages block the send buffer, introduce latency for other subscribers, and can cause memory pressure. Use HTTP for large payloads; send only a reference ID over WebSocket.

antipattern.description: Subscription state keyed by socket object rather than user ID, causing authorization to leak across reconnects.
antipattern.reason: On reconnect, a new socket is created. If subscriptions are keyed by socket, old subscriptions linger. If keyed by user ID without revoking old sockets, a user might receive messages intended for a different session.
